title: The Unless Statement 🚫
description: A Ruby-specific feature that makes negative conditions read like English.

slides.title: Reverse Logic ⏪
slides.content:
Sometimes you want to run code only if a condition is **false**.

You *could* use an `if` statement with a `!=` (not equal) operator.

But Ruby gives us a much more elegant, readable tool: the `unless` keyword.

`unless` literally means `if not`.

slides.title: Unless in Action
slides.content: Read the code aloud. It sounds exactly like plain English! "Unless you are tired, let's go running!"
slides.codeSnippet:
tired = false

unless tired
  puts "Let's go running!"
end

quizData.question: When does the code inside an `unless` block run?
quizData.explanation: `unless` runs its block *only* if the condition is false. It's the exact opposite of `if`.

slides.title: Single-Line If/Unless 📏
slides.content:
If you only have one line of code to run, Ruby lets you put the entire `if` or `unless` statement on a single line!

You just put the condition *after* the action.

```ruby
puts "You win!" if score == 100
```
It doesn't even need an `end` keyword!

slides.title: One-Liners
slides.content: These are incredibly popular in Ruby because they look incredibly clean.
slides.codeSnippet:
hungry = false

# Single line syntax:
puts "Eating a snack!" if hungry
puts "Going to the gym!" unless hungry

quizData.question: What happens if you use the one-line syntax: `puts "Hi" if false`?

options.text: It crashes because there is no 'end' keyword.

quizData.explanation: Because the condition is false, the action is skipped. And one-liners do not need an `end` keyword!

slides.title: Pro Tip 🚀
slides.content:
Don't chain `else` with an `unless`. It becomes instantly confusing to read. 
If you need an `else`, just use a regular `if...else` block instead.
